summary:
The placenta detaches from its central point upward but not with part of itself presenting maternally; external signs include an extended umbilical cord and higher uterus rather than exposed placental tissue facing outwardly during delivery.
The primary misconception here pertains specifically to where or how much of the placenta can be observed directly by the mother post-separation – only internal detachment occurs without significant exterior exposure beyond normal labor progressions such as cervical changes and fetal descent. To clarify further would require addressing each remaining detail about actual physiological processes involved versus visual misinterpretations under stress conditions typical for childbirth
